fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 79. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 74.

course_description.character: The official gran fondo of the Volta ao Algarve, scheduled as part of the professional stage race's event programme, but its host town has moved repeatedly — Tavira, Loulé, Lagoa, and Lagos across various editions. The 2026 tenth edition ran from Lagos. Despite chip timing, category rankings, and trophies, the organizer's own regulations explicitly define the event as non-competitive with no official classification — a mixed positioning that doesn't match the timed-race look and feel most riders expect. Roads remain open to ordinary traffic throughout.
course_description.signature_challenge: There is no organizer-published signature climb for the current Lagos-based course — earlier editions used different Algarve climbs (Fóia, Picota, Malhão, Barranco do Velho), but the route changes with the host town, so these should not be assumed to still be on course.
suffering_zones: Open-road sections — no closed-course protection, so normal traffic rules and priority apply throughout
Late-course pacing — riders must maintain at least 20 km/h at checkpoints and finish within 5 hours 30 minutes or risk being pulled by sweep vehicles

logistics.lodging_strategy: Abundant in Algarve tourist region including Lagoa, Lagos, Portimão; book early due to concurrent pro race.

biased_opinion.summary: Algarve Gran Fondo carries real pedigree — it's the official gran fondo of the Volta ao Algarve, Portugal's top professional stage race, now in its tenth edition (with a two-year pandemic hiatus along the way). But read the regulations, not just the branding: despite chip timing, rankings, and a yellow-jersey-style leader's award, the organizer explicitly calls the event non-competitive with no official classification. The course itself has moved repeatedly — Tavira, Loulé, Lagoa, and now Lagos for 2026 — so 'Algarve Gran Fondo' describes a changing route, not a fixed one. Entry is genuinely affordable (€40-€50) and the Volta connection gives it real atmosphere, but the mixed competitive/non-competitive positioning and shifting course are honest weaknesses to know going in.
strengths: Official gran fondo of the Volta ao Algarve — genuine pro-race pedigree and programme placement
Tenth edition in 2026, with a real multi-year track record despite a pandemic-era hiatus
Inexpensive relative to comparable European gran fondos: €40-€50 including insurance, feeds, medical/mechanical support, and a finisher medal
Two distances (130 km Gran Fondo, 90 km Mediofondo) let riders self-select
weaknesses: Officially non-competitive despite chip timing, rankings, and trophies — the regulations explicitly state there is no official classification, which may disappoint riders expecting a fully sanctioned race
Roads remain open to ordinary traffic for the full course
Course and host town change by edition — Tavira, Loulé, Lagoa, and Lagos have all hosted it, so named climbs and route character from past editions may not carry over
Restrictive refund policy: only 50% recoverable before a set date, then entries are final

Course documentation has minor inconsistencies across secondary sources — a third-party route-mapping link associated with the event shows a higher raw ascent figure than the official regulations' 2,213m, though the official figure is the more defensible one
biased_opinion.bottom_line: Ride it for the Volta ao Algarve atmosphere and the affordable price, not for a certified competitive result — the organizer's own rules say this is a well-supported, non-competitive ride wearing a race's trophies and rankings.

final_verdict.one_liner: Official amateur counterpart to Portugal's premier pro race, with pro climbs and Algarve beauty.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you want an affordable, well-supported ride tied to a real professional stage race atmosphere. Skip if you specifically want certified competitive results — the organizer's own regulations define this as non-competitive.
final_verdict.alternatives: If you want a genuinely competitive UCI-format Iberian gran fondo instead: The Millars (Castellón, Spain). If you want fixed, stable course character year to year: look for an Algarve gran fondo with a permanent host town.

citations.label: Official 2026 Regulations — Non-Competitive Classification, Cutoffs, Refunds

videos.title: 45ª Volta Algarve Bicicleta | Faro
videos.description:
Na última etapa da Volta ao Algarve em Bicicleta, na sua 45ª Edição, Tadej Pogačar partiu para a jornada final em posse da Camisola Amarela Turismo do Algarve, de comandante da geral, da Camisola Azul Águas do Algarve, de rei dos trepadores, e da Camisola Branca 120 Anos da FPC, de melhor jovem. 

O francês Arnaud Démare era o dono da Camisola Vermelha Cofidis, dos pontos. A Team Sunweb comanda por equipas. A Volta ao Algarve terminou no domingo dia 24 de Fevereiro com a quinta etapa, uma ligação de 173,5 quilómetros, que começou em Faro, às 12h30, e terminou no alto do Malhão, concelho de Loulé, cerca das 17h00, na segunda ascensão àquela mítica montanha do ciclismo português.

Algarve Granfondo Cofidis
Além da multidão que estava a apoiar os corredores no Malhão, domingo foi também dia de um grande evento popular, o Algarve Granfondo Cofidis, que juntou cerca de 870 ciclistas amadores. Foi uma prova aberta com partida e chegada em Loulé.

Esta iniciativa teve cariz solidário, uma vez que a Cofidis ofereceu uma árvore por cada participante. Deste modo, foram oferecidas 870 árvores para ajudar a reflorestar a serra de Monchique, afetada pelos incêndios de 2018.

Volta ao Algarve inclusiva
A última etapa da Volta ao Algarve contou ainda com uma demonstração de paraciclismo para cegos. Quatro deficientes visuais partiram à frente do pelotão, em tandens. 
Um dos guias foi o presidente da Federação Portuguesa de Ciclismo, Delmino Pereira.

A Mais Algarve, esteve na partida desta última etapa que ligou Faro ao Alto do Malhão, no concelho de Loulé e traz-lhe agora algumas das imagens dos bastidores e partidas, captadas pelo nosso repórter de imagem Paulo Ribeiro.
_________________________________________________________________
In the last stage of the Tour of the Algarve by bicycle, Tadej Pogačar left for the final day in possession of the Yellow Tourism Camel of the Algarve, commander of the general, Camisole Azul Águas do Algarve, king of the climbers, and the White shirt 120 Years of the FPC, of better youth.

Frenchman Arnaud Démare was the owner of the Red Sweater Cofidis, of the points. Team Sunweb commands teams. The Tour of the Algarve ended on Sunday, February 24 with the fifth stage, a 173.5-kilometre link, which began in Faro at 12:30 p.m., and ended at the top of Malhão, Loulé municipality, about 5:00 p.m., on the second ascent to that mythical mountain of Portuguese cycling.

Algarve Granfondo Cofidis
Besides the crowd that was supporting the runners in Malhão, Sunday was also the day of a big popular event, the Algarve Granfondo Cofidis, which gathered around 870 amateur cyclists. It was an open race with departure and arrival in Loulé.

This initiative was supportive since Cofidis offered one tree for each participant. In this way, 870 trees were offered to help reforest the Monchique mountain range, affected by the fires of 2018.

Back to the inclusive Algarve
The last leg of the Tour of the Algarve also had a demonstration of paracyclism for the blind. Four visually impaired departed at the head of the platoon, in tandem.
One of the guides was the president of the Portuguese Cycling Federation, Delmino Pereira.

The “Mais Algarve”, was in the departure of this last stage that linked Faro to Alto do Malhão, in the county of Loulé and now brings you some of the backstage images and matches, captured by our image reporter Paulo Ribeiro.

www.maisalgarve.pt
